title: Introduction. Grapes are gaining popularity as a healthful food due to their potential to reduce the risk of metabolic syndrome and its complications, such as type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular diseases. As the public health burden of metabolic syndrome increases, functional foods rich in polyphenolics and other bioactive natural products show significant translational potential. This includes promoting grape consumption for its possible health benefits.

sentences.text: Introduction.
Grapes are gaining popularity as a healthful food due to their potential to reduce the risk of metabolic syndrome and its complications, such as type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular diseases.
As the public health burden of metabolic syndrome increases, functional foods rich in polyphenolics and other bioactive natural products show significant translational potential.
This includes promoting grape consumption for its possible health benefits.
One in ten adults in the USA and worldwide has developed metabolic syndrome.
This is characterized by clusters of biochemical events in the body that can lead to various complications and diseases.
Metabolic syndrome encompasses phenotypes such as obesity, systemic inflammation, hyperglycemia and insulin resistance, and hyperlipidemia.
These conditions significantly elevate the risk of developing type 2 diabetes mellitus and cardiovascular disease.
It is not only related to cardiovascular diseases but may also be connected to dysfunctions in the kidneys, liver, muscles, and other organs.
While the factors causing metabolic syndrome are well characterized, targeted molecular-level analysis is still underway to elucidate possible gene regulation or enzyme functions.
Several genes related to hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia, obesity, hypertension, and insulin resistance have been identified and studied to date.
However, it remains unclear how bioactive metabolites from grapes might help prevent or treat metabolic syndrome through direct intervention or dietary changes.
Research indicates that the regular consumption of fruits may help prevent and manage various risk factors associated with metabolic syndrome and its complications.
Adopting optimal dietary habits can be a learned way to prevent or manage type 2 diabetes mellitus, one of the most prevalent noncommunicable diseases.
It is noteworthy that the full metabolic profiling, including grape metabolomics data, is not yet fully unraveled.

Understanding the diversity of phytochemicals, the predominant polyphenolics, their interactions with other molecules, and potential drug interactions is crucial for guiding dietary recommendations and enhancing therapeutic efficacy.
This knowledge can offer new and innovative insights that could influence clinical dietary counseling and public health strategies.

Studying the metabolic fate of grapes with complex phytochemical ingredients is particularly challenging because the exact composition and active molecules are usually unknown and the absorption and metabolism of these phytochemicals in organisms may be highly variable.
Research into the bioavailability and bioactivity of grape metabolites in animals and humans is ongoing.
Several studies aim to understand how these compounds are absorbed, metabolized, and utilized in the body.

While the potential health benefits of grapes and their major bioactive polyphenols, such as resveratrol, have been widely discussed, more research is needed to confirm their efficacy in managing metabolic syndrome.

In this context, the interplay between bioactive metabolites, gene expression, and phenotype is essential for unraveling the complex mechanisms underlying grape-mediated protection against metabolic syndrome.
Studies in rodents have shown that consuming grape products affects multiple factors, including metabolic activity and subsequent metabolic adaptation, in an animal model of metabolic syndrome.

Determining the effects of grape intake on laboratory variables with several known markers, including fasting glucose, insulin, cholesterol, triglycerides, and C-reactive protein, functions including blood pressure, systolic function, and diastolic function, and specific genes related to antioxidant defense and inflammation can illuminate how grape metabolites may help prevent conditions associated with metabolic syndrome.
To gain a comprehensive understanding, the next stage involves using advanced statistical and modeling approaches to assess the interactions among the measured variables.
